text.title: Async Resource Lifecycle
text.summary: App-scoped clients, async context managers, background task tracking, and CPU offload patterns.

rule.statement: Create httpx.AsyncClient, DB pools, and Redis clients once at application startup; never instantiate them per request or inside loops.
rule.rationale: Creating a client per request leaks sockets, breaks HTTP/2 connection reuse, and exhausts file descriptors under load.

rule.statement: Implement resource cleanup via async context managers (asynccontextmanager or __aenter__/__aexit__); never rely on garbage collection for async resources.
rule.rationale: GC does not call async close() methods; connections and file handles leak silently without explicit context management.

example.code:
from contextlib import asynccontextmanager
@asynccontextmanager async def get_db_session(): session = await create_session() try: yield session await session.commit() except Exception: await session.rollback() raise finally: await session.close()

rule.statement: Store all fire-and-forget tasks in a set and register a done callback to remove them; never discard the task reference immediately after create_task().
rule.rationale: Unreferenced tasks may be garbage-collected before completion; exceptions are silently swallowed.

rule.statement: Run CPU-bound operations in loop.run_in_executor(None, fn) for tasks under ~50ms; use ProcessPoolExecutor for longer tasks.
rule.rationale: A CPU-bound synchronous call inside async def blocks the event loop for all concurrent tasks for its full duration.

example.code:
from concurrent.futures import ProcessPoolExecutor
_pool = ProcessPoolExecutor(max_workers=4)
async def process_image(data: bytes) -> bytes: loop = asyncio.get_running_loop() return await loop.run_in_executor(_pool, cpu_transform, data)
